Technology

ESA & Vibration Monitoring

Complementary methods. Different physics. Full fleet coverage.

Vibration is excellent for accessible rotating equipment. But 30-40% of a typical fleet sits beyond sensor reach — submerged pumps, sealed drives, hazardous zones. ESA monitors those assets from the motor control cabinet. The question isn't which is better. It's which assets need which method.

Request a Demo See Detection Matrix ↓

Side by side

Each method detects through different physics. The question is which assets need which method.

Criteria ESA (SAM4) Vibration Monitoring
Sensor location Motor control cabinet (MCC) On or near the asset
Physical access Not required Required
Installation time <60 minutes per machine Hours to days
Production shutdown Brief de-energisation at MCC. Back online same shift. Often required for mounting
Time to first insight 2 days to 2 weeks (baseline calibration) Immediate (first measurement)
Monitoring mode Continuous 24/7 Continuous or periodic (route-based)
Submerged assets Monitored from MCC Cannot mount sensor
ATEX/hazardous zones No ATEX cert needed Requires ATEX-rated equipment
Enclosed/sealed assets No disassembly needed Requires access to housing
Bearing fault detection Detected High resolution
Misalignment/imbalance Detected High resolution
Electrical faults Primary strength Not detectable
Process deviations Cavitation, clogging, efficiency Limited
Fleet scalability Rapid deployment (1,000+ assets) Per-asset installation
Cost per monitoring point Lower (MCC install) Higher for hard-to-reach assets

Beyond vibration's reach

ESA's defining advantage is reach. These scenarios add coverage that vibration physically cannot provide.

Submerged or enclosed

Submersible pumps, sealed compressors, enclosed blowers. ESA monitors from the control cabinet. No disassembly.

Hazardous zones

ATEX areas, confined spaces, flammable atmospheres. ESA sensors stay in the MCC. No explosion risk.

Remote sites

Off-grid pumping stations, remote production lines. ESA transmits via cellular. No site visits.

Electrical faults

Stator winding degradation, rotor bar defects, insulation breakdown. ESA detects these. Vibration cannot.

Between vibration rounds

Continuous 24/7 monitoring catches faults between scheduled vibration routes.

Fleet-scale speed

Deploy across 1,000+ assets in weeks. MCC installation <60 minutes per asset.

Where vibration has the edge

Vibration monitoring has decades of field validation. For certain faults on accessible assets, it provides resolution ESA doesn't match.

High-resolution mechanical diagnostics

Vibration sensors mounted on machinery provide direct mechanical data. Bearing defect frequencies, gear mesh patterns, resonance analysis. For accessible assets, vibration delivers finer spectral detail.

Established standards

Decades of ISO 10816, ISO 20816 validation. Industry standards. Widespread expertise. If your fleet is 60-70% accessible, mature vibration is proven and familiar.

Caveat: These advantages apply to accessible assets. Once a motor sits beyond physical reach, you need ESA.

Fault-by-fault detection matrix

This is a coverage map, not a scorecard. Use both where they add value.

Select detection method by failure mode and asset accessibility, not by vendor preference. Where both methods show strength, the primary method depends on whether the asset is physically reachable.

Fault Class ESA (SAM4) Vibration Primary Method Rationale Applicability Constraints
Bearing damage Moderate Strong Both (complementary) Vibration provides higher spectral resolution on accessible assets. ESA detects bearing faults on submerged or enclosed assets where vibration cannot be mounted. ESA detection rate: 45.9% overall. Vibration preferred when asset is physically reachable.
Misalignment / imbalance Strong Strong Vibration (when accessible) Both methods detect the torque and load variations caused by mechanical misalignment. Vibration provides directional data (axial vs radial). ESA adds value on inaccessible assets or as a second confirmation signal.
Stator winding degradation Strong Not detectable ESA only Winding faults produce current asymmetry and harmonic shifts visible only in the electrical signature. No mechanical vibration produced until late-stage failure. ESA is the only non-invasive continuous method for this fault class.
Rotor bar defects Strong Limited ESA primary Broken rotor bars produce characteristic sideband frequencies around the supply frequency. Vibration may detect secondary mechanical effects but not the root cause. ESA is the standard detection method for rotor bar faults in induction motors.
Supply asymmetry Strong Not detectable ESA only Voltage and current imbalance between phases is an electrical condition with no mechanical signature. Only visible in the power supply data. Relevant for all three-phase motors. Common root cause of premature winding failure.
Cavitation Strong Moderate ESA primary Cavitation causes load fluctuations that modulate the motor current signature. Vibration detects acoustic effects but often only at advanced stages. ESA detects earlier-stage cavitation on submerged pumps where vibration sensors cannot be mounted.
Clogging / blockage Strong Limited ESA primary Blockage changes the load profile and operating point of the pump. ESA detects the shift in power draw and current waveform. Vibration has limited sensitivity to hydraulic changes. 97.4% detection rate across 545 confirmed cases. Strongest ESA fault class.
Efficiency loss Strong Not detectable ESA only Efficiency degradation is a power and energy measurement. No mechanical vibration signal produced. ESA tracks energy consumption and operating point deviation continuously. Requires baseline calibration period (2 days to 2 weeks) to establish reference efficiency.
Belt / coupling wear Moderate Strong Vibration primary Belt and coupling faults produce distinctive vibration patterns (belt frequency harmonics, coupling angular misalignment). Vibration provides direct mechanical measurement. Vibration preferred when sensor access to the coupling or belt guard is feasible.
Loose foundation Limited Strong Vibration primary Foundation looseness produces characteristic sub-harmonic vibration patterns. This is a structural condition best measured at the machine base. Vibration sensors on the machine base or foundation bolt provide the clearest signal.
Gear mesh faults Limited Strong Vibration primary Gear mesh frequencies and their sidebands are high-frequency mechanical phenomena. Vibration provides direct, high-resolution measurement of gear condition. Vibration is the established standard for gearbox diagnostics on accessible assets.

Better together

100% of your fleet. Not 60–70%.

60–70%

Accessible assets covered by vibration

30–40%

Previously invisible assets now monitored by ESA

Vibration and ESA detect faults through different physics. Vibration reads mechanical motion. ESA reads electrical current signature. When both methods cover the same asset, you get two independent signals. The overlap isn't duplication — it's validation.

Vibration: direct mechanical data from accessible assets.

ESA: full drivetrain data from the motor control cabinet.

Combined: coverage across the entire fleet.

When to use what

Select by asset condition and failure mode, not by vendor preference.

Use ESA when

  • Assets are submerged, enclosed, or in hazardous zones
  • Physical sensor access is not feasible
  • Fleet-wide coverage is needed at speed
  • Electrical fault detection is required
  • Continuous monitoring between vibration routes adds value

Use vibration when

  • Physical access to the asset is available
  • High-resolution mechanical diagnostics are needed (gear mesh, bearing envelope, foundation)
  • ISO 10816/20816 compliance is required

Evidence from the field

Schiphol Airport

What happened

Bearing degradation developing on baggage handling drives. Vibration sensors on the same assets showed no alarm.

How ESA detected it

Current signature analysis from the MCC identified characteristic bearing defect frequencies in the electrical signal.

Outcome

All 9 confirmed faults detected during deployment. Maintenance scheduled before breakdown.

9 / 9 faults detected
"At first I was a bit skeptical about SAM4's ability to detect failures, but the results speak for themselves: It just works."
Pieter Bakker, Baggage Service Manager, Schiphol Airport

DuPont

What happened

Mature vibration programme in place across the site. ESA found faults vibration had not flagged on pumps with no redundancy.

How ESA detected it

Continuous current monitoring detected developing issues months before failure on assets running 24/7.

Outcome

Detections prevented unplanned shutdowns worth €1.7M over three years.

€1.7M costs prevented
"We invested heavily in condition-based maintenance — vibration, oil analysis, airflow, the lot. But those approaches can still be snapshots. SAM4 added continuous visibility, and that changes how confident you feel about what's happening."
John McCrystal, Reliability and Maintenance Specialist, DuPont

Common questions from reliability teams

No. SAM4 extends your monitoring programme to assets vibration can't reach. Submerged pumps, sealed blowers, ATEX zones, remote sites. Where vibration delivers value, keep it. SAM4 covers the 30–40% with zero visibility. Many customers run both.

Not everything. For high-resolution mechanical diagnostics on accessible assets, vibration provides finer spectral detail. ESA adds electrical faults and process deviations that vibration doesn't see.

Good. That means 60–70% of your fleet is covered. SAM4 targets the remaining 30–40%. It also adds continuous monitoring between vibration routes.

Every alert is reviewed by a reliability engineer before delivery. 0.32% false communication rate across 1,874 resolved incidents — fewer than 1 in 300.

See what ESA detects on your assets

Request a demo and speak with a reliability engineer within 48 hours. Real data from real assets. No slides.
